stem: In a advertising study comparing Platform A vs. Platform B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher conversion rate (66.7% vs 54.3%). However, when stratified by product category: In low-consideration products (n_X=20, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 81% vs Treatment Y's 72%. In high-consideration products (n_X=120, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 50% vs Treatment Y's 44%. The true population proportion of low-consideration products is uncertain, estimated between 36% and 49%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to high-consideration products (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.071, 0.075], point estimate: 0.072. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.2 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=54.3%, Y=66.7% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In low-consideration products: X=81% > Y=72%. In high-consideration products: X=50% > Y=44%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Platform A is applied more to high-consideration products (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
